content:
# Paleoecology and Paleoenvironmental Reconstruction of the Lower Silurian Qusaiba Shale in the Qasim Region

**Summary**: The thesis investigates the paleoecology and paleoenvironment of the Lower Silurian Qusaiba Shale in the Qasim Region using an integrated approach of foraminiferal morphogroup analysis, lithofacies characterization, and geochemical data. It identifies a diverse assemblage of agglutinated foraminifera and reconstructs environmental changes from offshore to lower shoreface settings.

---

## Research Problem

The study addresses the need to characterize the paleoenvironmental evolution of the Qusaiba Formation during the Aeronian, specifically investigating how detrital flux, paleoproductivity, and oxygenation levels influenced foraminiferal assemblages in a clastic setting.

## Objectives

- Identification of the foraminiferal species and biofacies in the gray-warm shale unit of the Qusaiba and Sharawa formations of the Qalibah Group in outcrop
- Analysis of foraminiferal morphotypes and inferred life style (i.e., epifaunal, shallow infaunal, deep infaunal) based on morphogroup interpretations.
- Determining the temporal succession of paleo-oxygenation based on major and trace elements.
- Reconstructing the paleoecology & paleoenvironment of the middle to upper Qusaiba Formation in the Qasim Region.
- Characterize the foraminiferal assemblage and diversity in the Qusaiba Shale.
- Determine the graptolite biozonation of the Lower Silurian succession.

## Methods

The study employed biostratigraphy using graptolites to establish a biozone (L. convolutus). Foraminiferal analysis included morphotype classification and wall texture characterization. Geochemical analysis involved XRD to determine mineral percentages (Quartz, K-feldspar, Clay minerals) and XRF for major element oxides and trace elements (V, Cr, Mo, Co, Fe, Mn, P, Ni, Zn, Ba). Proxy calculations were performed using ratios such as V/Cr, Mo/Al, Co/Al, Fe/Al for redox; P/Ti, Ba/Al, Ni/1, Zn/Al for productivity; and Ti/Al, Zr/Al, and Quartz content for detrital flux. Framboidal pyrite size distribution (>65 μm) was used as a secondary oxygenation indicator.

## Data and Materials

Two outcrop sections in the Qusaiba depression; 5312 foraminiferal specimens from the upper Qusaiba and lower Sharawra formations; XRD mineralogy data; XRF elemental concentrations; SEM-EDS mapping; lithofacies descriptions of four distinct units.

## Key Findings

- The study identified a diverse assemblage of 77 species across 24 genera of agglutinated foraminifera, the first such record in clastic deposits from the Gondwana paleocontinent. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 54]])
- Lithofacies Mdg (gray to dark gray mudstone) was characterized by 36-55% quartz and 30-48% clay minerals (predominantly kaolinite), interpreted as an offshore environment. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 45]])
- Lithofacies Mlg (light gray mudstone) showed the highest graptolite abundance, including Normalograptus scalaris and Pristiograptus reckardsi, indicating a shallower offshore environment. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 46]])
- Lithofacies Mzr contained 3-10% gypsum (confirmed by XRD), interpreted as a transition between offshore and lower shoreface environments. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 48]])
- Lithofacies Sfc 1 and Sfc 2 were identified as lower shoreface environment deposits, with Sfc 2 showing hummocky cross-stratification. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 50]])
- Foraminiferal morphogroup A (subgroups A1 and A2) was prevalent throughout the succession but peaked in the muddy parts of the lower shoreface environment. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 64]])
- Morphogroup B3, including Thuramminoides and Psammosphaera(?), were the most dominant types found in this investigation. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 65]])
- Foraminifera with a rough wall surface (e.g., Raibosammina aspera) were concentrated in the upper part of the Mzr and Sfc facies, while those with smooth wall surfaces were more common in lower sections. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 68]])
- The study identified a diverse foraminiferal assemblage of 24 genera and 77 species, predominantly monochambered forms (>95%). ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 107]])
- Graptolite analysis placed the succession in the L. convolutus biozone. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 79]])
- Quartz content in the Qusaiba shale ranges from 36% to 64% (average 52.17%), indicating strong detrital supply. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 83]])
- Total clay content varies from 21% to 48% (mean 29.29%), with kaolinite being the dominant mineral. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 86]])
- The detrital proxy Ti/Al and Zr/Al showed declining trends toward the facies Mdg, coinciding with lower foraminiferal abundance. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 97]])
- Paleoredox indicators (V/Cr, Co/Al, Fe/Al) showed a major change at the base of facies Mdg, indicating a transition to dysoxic conditions. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 98]])
- Large framboidal pyrite (>65 μm) was recovered in the basal part (facies Mdg), supporting a dysoxic environment. ([[2018-perdana|Perdana, 2018, p. 102]])

## Limitations

The study notes that some graptolite zones were 'undefined' due to poor preservation in sand-dominated lithologies and suggests additional TOC, Th, and U analysis to improve paleoenvironmental interpretation.

## Recommendations and Future Work

- Conduct TOC, Th, and U analysis to complement foraminiferal and elemental data.
- Extend investigation to both outcrops and subsurface to study Ammobaculites-type forms.
- Develop a well-established Silurian foraminiferal biozonation in Saudi Arabia for use in biosteering.
